responses: We built an AI system that evaluates software architecture proposals before they enter the formal design review process. The system reads architecture decision records — ADRs — and produces a structured evaluation covering: does this ADR address its stated constraints? What alternatives should the author consider? What failure modes are not addressed? Is the scope of impact correctly estimated? The system is used by engineering leads as a pre-filter before they invest time in synchronous reviews. The problem it solves is that we were spending 4-6 hours per week per engineering lead in design reviews where basic problems — undefined failure modes, unconsidered alternatives, underestimated blast radius — had not been addressed. Post-deployment over 8 months: average time-to-review-complete has dropped by 44%, and post-implementation defect rate for reviewed ADRs is down 31% compared to the prior 8-month period.

We evaluated three approaches: a structured checklist tool that required authors to answer specific questions before submission; a retrieval-augmented system using our historical ADR library as a reference corpus; and a general LLM evaluation with a structured rubric. We rejected the checklist tool because it added friction for authors without providing any analytical value — authors would answer the questions without genuine engagement. The RAG approach using our ADR library was appealing for grounding evaluations in our specific context, but our ADR library was too small (fewer than 200 documents) and inconsistently formatted to provide reliable retrieval. We chose the LLM with structured rubric because it could apply consistent evaluation criteria without requiring a large corpus and could be calibrated by updating the rubric. The explicit thing we gave up is the ability to evaluate an ADR against our specific architectural history — the model does not know what decisions we have made before and cannot identify when a proposal contradicts a prior decision.

We identified four failure modes with mitigation strategies. First: the model evaluates a high-quality ADR negatively because it is unfamiliar with our internal architectural patterns, creating friction for experienced engineers who have context the model lacks. Mitigation: we include a summary of our current architectural principles in the system prompt, updated quarterly; engineering leads can override evaluations with a written justification that goes into the ADR record. Second: the model produces a positive evaluation of a fundamentally flawed ADR, and the ADR proceeds through review without catching the flaw. Mitigation: the AI evaluation is explicitly framed as a pre-filter, not a gate — the synchronous review still occurs; the evaluation is one input, not a decision. Third: engineering leads begin deferring to the AI evaluation rather than forming their own judgment, reducing review quality. Mitigation: we track review comment diversity as a proxy for independent thinking; if comment patterns converge toward AI evaluation language, we plan to rotate the system off for a quarter. Fourth: rubric calibration drift — the rubric becomes misaligned with actual architectural standards as our stack evolves. Mitigation: the rubric is reviewed and updated during each quarterly architecture review.

The most significant lesson from this build is about the relationship between automation and judgment. In month 3, we noticed that engineering leads were spending less time on the pre-review ADRs — time in the synchronous review dropped from 4 hours to 2 hours on average. We initially counted this as a success. Then in month 5, we found that 3 architectural decisions had been approved with failure modes that the AI evaluation had flagged but that reviewers had not followed up on — the AI flag existed in the record but had been marked as addressed without documentation. The system had created a false sense of coverage. We added a required written response for every AI-flagged issue, and changed our success metric from review time reduction to post-implementation defect rate. The AI had confidently flagged issues in a way that created the appearance of rigor without producing it. We had to redesign the workflow to make the AI output actionable rather than informational.

rationale: High L4. Clarity earns 5 — precise purpose with measurable outcomes, stakeholder alignment, and explicit before/after metrics. Tradeoff earns 4 — three alternatives with explicit naming of what the chosen approach gives up. Failure modes are well-structured with mitigations. Blast radius stays 3 (scope identified but no quantified containment). Reflection earns 3 — specific lessons, process changes, but not systemic evolution in methodology. Override evidence earns 3 — a specific pattern of misplaced AI confidence is identified with a redesigned workflow.
